revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23985667504|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Haibutsu kishaku]][[de:Haibutsu kishaku]][[en:Haibutsu kishaku]][[es:Haibutsu kishaku]][[fa:هایبوتسو کیشاکو]][[fi:Haibutsu kishaku]][[fr:Haibutsu kishaku]][[id:Haibutsu kishaku]][[ja:廃仏毀釈]][[ko:폐불훼석]][[pl:Haibutsu-kishaku]][[pt:Haibutsu kishaku]][[th:ไฮบุตสึคิชากุ]][[zh:廢佛毀釋]]
{{Short description|Movement that advocate the expulsion of Buddhism from Japan}} {{Infobox Chinese | kyujitai = 廢佛毀釋 | shinjitai = 廃仏毀釈 | kana = はいぶつきしゃく | revhep = Haibutsu kishaku }}
[[File:Burning-of-sutras-and-religious-objects.jpg|thumb|275 px|The burning of [[:en:Sūtras|Sūtras]] during the ''haibutsu kishaku'']]{{Nihongo|'''''Haibutsu kishaku'''''|廃仏毀釈}} (literally "abolish {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism in Japan}} and destroy [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shākyamuni]]") is a term that indicates a current of thought continuous in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}'s history which advocates the expulsion of Buddhism from Japan.<ref name="EOS.HK" >[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1354 Encyclopedia of Shinto - Haibutsu Kishaku] accessed on March 15, 2008</ref> More narrowly, it also indicates a particular historic movement and specific historic events based on that ideology which, during the [[Meiji Restoration]], produced the destruction of Buddhist temples, images and texts, and the forced return to secular life of Buddhist monks.<ref name="EOS.HK"/> It is a {{ill|Yojijukugo|en|Yojijukugo|qid=Q7010524|lt=Yojijukugo|1=Yojijukugo}} phrase.
== ''Haibutsu kishaku'' in history == An early example of ''haibutsu kishaku'' is the [[Mononobe clan]]'s {{ill|Persecution of Buddhists|en|Persecution of Buddhists|qid=Q1000923|lt=anti-Buddhist|1=Persecution of Buddhists}} policies during the time of Buddhism's early introduction to Japan in the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="EOS.HK"/> The Mononobe were opposed to the spread of Buddhism not on religious grounds, but rather because of nationalism and xenophobia. The [[Nakatomi clan]], ancestors of the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]], were allies of the Mononobe in their opposition to Buddhism.
Another example is the policies of temple closure and monk defrocking of the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en|Okayama Domain|qid=Q903497|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama Domain}}, {{ill|Aizu Domain|en|Aizu Domain|qid=Q4389944|lt=Aizu|1=Aizu Domain}}, and {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|qid=Q1146904|lt=Mito Domain|1=Mito Domain}}s, also adopted for political and economic, rather than religious, reasons during the early modern period.<ref name="EOS.HK"/> These [[:en:Han (country subdivision)|domain]]al policies were in general based on {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|qid=Q9581|lt=Confucian|1=Confucianism}} anti-Buddhist thought.<ref name="EOS.HK"/> The Meiji period form of ''haibutsu kishaku'', based on {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|qid=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}} and Shinto-centrism, was instead dictated by a desire to distinguish between foreign Buddhism and a purely Japanese Shinto.<ref name="EOS.HK"/>
== ''Haibutsu kishaku'' during the Meiji Restoration == [[File:Nagane Tanaka Haibutsu Kishaku.jpg|thumb|left|Temple bells being smelted for bronze during the ''haibutsu kishaku'']] [[File:1901, Kofukuji Temple, group record of damaged Buddha statues, old photograph.jpg|thumb|273x273px|A set of statues from Kōfuku-ji that fell into disrepair, including works by {{ill|Kaikei|en|Kaikei|qid=Q3191958|lt=Kaikei|1=Kaikei}}. Many of these statues were picked up by collectors and are displayed around the world.]] The ''haibutsu kishaku'' during the [[Meiji Restoration]], the most famous instance of the phenomenon, was an event triggered by the official policy of separation of Shinto and Buddhism (or ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'') <ref name="EOS.HK"/> that after 1868 caused great damage to Buddhism in Japan. The destruction of Buddhist property took place on a large scale all over the country. For example, [[Kōfuku-ji]] in Nara suffered greatly. The temple, which is now a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}, was hit with full force by the movement. The temple's land was seized, the priests forced to become Shinto priests, walls were torn down, trees were planted, and the area became part of one of the city's parks.<ref>{{cite web |website=www.japannavigator.com |url=http://www.japannavigator.com/junrei/reijo/13-ban.htm |title=Kofukuji, Dream of a Temple |access-date=6 December 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090205180554/http://www.japannavigator.com/junrei/reijo/13-ban.htm |archive-date=2009-02-05}}</ref> The anti-Buddhist riots caused damage to all large temples of the city.<ref>[http://www.city.nara.nara.jp/b_hp/english/kokon/rekishi/index.htm Nara City Home Page, History of Nara] accessed on March 15, 2008 {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080103044856/http://www.city.nara.nara.jp/b_hp/english/kokon/rekishi/index.htm |date=January 3, 2008 }}</ref> The violence marked every region of the country permanently. Between 1872 and 1874, 18 thousand temples were eradicated, and maybe as many again from 1868 to 1872.<ref name="Breen"/> Japanologist Martin Collcutt believes Japanese Buddhism was on the verge of total eradication.<ref name="Breen">{{cite book | last = John Breen | author-link = John Breen (scholar) |editor=Mark Teeuwen |editor-link=Mark Teeuwen | title = Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | date = July 2000 | page = 230 | location = Honolulu | isbn = 978-0-8248-2363-4 | oclc = 43487317 }}</ref>
The violence had just released pent-up popular anger at the Buddhists which had been brewing for centuries because of their close alliance with the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] in the [[Danka system|''danka'' system]],<ref>[http://www.hp.com/hpinfo/newsroom/press_kits/2006/kyoto/bg_foundation.pdf Releasing the Spirit of Kyoto: Historical Background]{{dead link|date=September 2013}} accessed on March 15, 2008</ref> an alliance from which the religion had derived immense benefit. Although the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'s official philosophy was lay {{ill|Neo-Confucianism|en|Neo-Confucianism|qid=Q825754|lt=Neo-Confucianism|1=Neo-Confucianism}},<ref>[http://www.willamette.edu/~rloftus/neoconfucianism.html Ronald Loftus, Willamette University - Neoconfucianism], accessed on March 15, 2008</ref> Buddhism had become an integral part of the state as a consequence of the Tokugawa's [[Christianity in Japan#History|anti-Christian policy]]. To stop the propagation of the [[:en:Christian religion|Christian religion]], they had introduced the ''danka'' system, which obliged families to affiliate themselves with a Buddhist temple<ref name="review">[https://web.archive.org/web/20120207151137/http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/history/itinerario/bookreviews-16.html Paul B. Watt, Review of ''Nam-Lin Hur, Death and Social Order in Tokugawa Japan: Buddhism, Anti-Christianity, and the Danka System''], Internet Archive</ref> and, in return, this would certify that they were not Christian. Without this certification a normal life in Tokugawa Japan was impossible.<ref name="review"/> Because of this, temples could and often would blackmail parishioners.<ref name="hur">Nam-Lin Hur, Death and social order in Tokugawa Japan: Buddhism, anti-Christianity, and the danka system, Harvard University Asia Center, 2007; pp. 1-30 (The Rise of Funerary Buddhism in Tokugawa Japan). [https://web.archive.org/web/20120331070517/http://www.fas.harvard.edu/~asiactr/publications/pdfs/Hur%20intro.pdf Internet archive]</ref> Under the [[danka system|''danka'' system]] families had by law several obligations towards Buddhist institutions, among them monetary donations to their affiliated temple.<ref name="review"/> Because there were some 100,000 temples in a country of 30 million people, on average 300 persons had to support a temple, so the burden was considerable.<ref name="hur"/> Buddhism, as an institution, had a vested interest in the shogunate system, and therefore became a target of all the parties working for the downfall of the shogunate.
There were definite political and economic motivations too, in that the domainal governments wanted to restore public finances at Buddhists's expense, and the ''shinbutsu bunri'' offered a pretext to appropriate Buddhist lands.<ref name="EOS.HK"/>
It is estimated that 40,000 Buddhist temples were destroyed in Japan during this disastrous nationwide anti-Buddhist mayhem, and in certain places the percentage of Buddhist temples destroyed reached 80%.<ref>{{cite book | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rz2wCQAAQBAJ&q=japan+destroyed+40,000+temples&pg=PA21 | title=Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism: Myōshinji, a living religion | author=Jørn Borup | date=25 February 2008 | page=21| publisher=BRILL | isbn=9789047433095 }}</ref>
It is difficult to estimate how many temples were closed during the turmoil, because it seems likely that many disappeared simply because Buddhist authorities, taking advantage of the fall of the Tokugawa, were trying to streamline the system and eliminate redundancies.<ref name="ananda">Jason Ānanda Josephson, When Buddhism Became a “Religion”, Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 33 (1), 143-168, 2006. [https://web.archive.org/web/20131229183011/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2889 Internet archive]</ref> Under the shogunate, obtaining the permission to open or close a temple had not been easy. However, the complete disappearance of Buddhist temples from domains like {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|qid=Q676187|lt=Satsuma|1=Satsuma Domain}} was indeed due to the ''haibutsu kishaku''.<ref name="ananda"/>
==See also== *{{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} *{{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan|1=Buddhism in Japan}} *[[Ko-Shintō|Koshintō]] *{{ill|Hujum|en|Hujum|qid=Q5935362|lt=Hujum|1=Hujum}} *[[:en:USSR anti-religious campaign (1928-1941)|USSR anti-religious campaign (1928-1941)]] *{{ill|Martyrs of Japan|en|Martyrs of Japan|qid=Q6777753|lt=Martyrs of Japan|1=Martyrs of Japan}} *{{ill|Persecution of Buddhists|en|Persecution of Buddhists|qid=Q1000923|lt=Persecution of Buddhists|1=Persecution of Buddhists}} *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *{{ill|Shinbutsu kakuri|en|Shinbutsu kakuri|qid=Q3482168|lt=Shinbutsu kakuri|1=Shinbutsu kakuri}} *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] *[[Tokugawa Nariaki]] *{{ill|Buddhist crisis|en|Buddhist crisis|qid=Q635624|lt=Buddhist crisis|1=Buddhist crisis}} *[[:en:Chittagong Hill Tracts Conflict|Chittagong Hill Tracts Conflict]] *[[Buddhas of Bamyan#Attacks on the Buddha's statue]] *{{ill|Iconoclasm|en|Iconoclasm|qid=Q181543|lt=Iconoclasm|1=Iconoclasm}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/43487317 OCLC 43487317] * James Ketelaar: Of Heretics and Martyrs in Meiji Japan: Buddhism and its Persecution, Princeton 1990 * Grapard, Allan G. (1992), [https://web.archive.org/web/20140403094657/http://enlight.lib.ntu.edu.tw/FULLTEXT/JR-MAG/mag133523.pdf Review of: James Ketelaar, Of Heretics and Martyrs in Meiji Japan]: Buddhism and Its Persecution, Princeton 1990. In: Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 19 (4), 390-395
